interactions.summary: PBD recounts a Chinese PR approach offering money routed through charity; Jiang explains Chinese soft power as elite co-optation based on the assumption that elites, not people, hold power.

PBD asks about Islam and Muslims in China. Jiang says terrorist attacks produced a crackdown, but the larger trend is Han nationalism and reduced minority language autonomy, including pressure toward Chinese over minority languages and English.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_01 is Patrick Bet-David or the PBD side of the interview; SPEAKER_00 is Jiang except for brief intro/audio fragments. Diarization is treated as a hint and the exchange context controls attribution.

claims.claim: Jiang says China’s soft-power approach is to co-opt influential media elites by treating them well, giving them access, and letting positive feeling translate into favorable commentary.
He says Muslim and Uyghur terrorist attacks generated a Chinese crackdown, but that crackdown sits inside a larger turn toward Han nationalism.
He says recent policy pushes Chinese language over minority dialects and is also reducing English instruction, though English remains popular with middle-class families seeking overseas options.

signature_moments.moment: Soft power is not persuasion of the public; it is bribing or charming the elites who shape what the public sees.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the PBD anecdote a reusable institutional model.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: China’s soft-power strategy of influencing high-status media or intellectual figures through hospitality, money, access, and selective exposure.
A policy and civilizational push toward Han Chinese identity at the expense of minority autonomy, language, and rights.
